serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Taiwan Mazesoba with Karaage is approximately 500 g, comprising a bowl of thick wheat noodles tossed in a rich soy-chili tare sauce topped with minced pork, an egg yolk, nori, green onions, and accompanied by several pieces of karaage fried chicken.

nutrient_source.title: Noodle dish with meat and sauce — composite estimation based on USDA FoodData Central entries for wheat noodles (cooked), Japanese-style fried chicken (karaage), and soy-based sauce components
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (Foundation Foods and SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Taiwan Mazesoba with Karaage as a composite dish. Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data: cooked wheat noodles (FDC ID 168936), Japanese-style fried chicken analogous to USDA fried chicken entries, soy sauce, chili oil, minced pork, and egg yolk. Proportions estimated for a typical restaurant serving (approximately 500 g total: ~200 g noodles, ~150 g karaage, ~80 g minced pork and sauce, ~70 g toppings). Per-100g values derived by weighted average across components. Confidence is medium due to composite estimation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of wheat noodles and mixed noodle dishes — analogous estimate based on cooked wheat noodles (GI ~54–58) from the University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Taiwan Mazesoba with Karaage. GI of 55 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked wheat noodles (GI range 54–58, medium category). The presence of protein (karaage, minced pork) and fat (chili oil, fried chicken) in the dish is expected to moderate the glycemic response relative to plain noodles. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI x carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (55 x 102.5) / 100 = 56.4, where carbohydrate per 500 g serving = 20.5 x 5 = 102.5 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy-based tare sauce, soy sauce, and seasoned minced pork
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid Taiwan Mazesoba with Karaage due to its estimated sodium content of approximately 520 mg per 100 g (approximately 2,600 mg per standard 500 g serving), which approaches or exceeds the recommended daily sodium limit of 2,000 mg in a single meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index wheat noodles with moderate carbohydrate load per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume Taiwan Mazesoba with Karaage in moderated portions, as the dish has a medium GI of approximately 55 and a high glycemic load per standard serving (~56), which can cause a significant postprandial blood glucose rise; pairing with non-starchy vegetables and reducing noodle portion size may help manage glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from deep-fried karaage and minced pork, combined with high sodium from soy-based sauce
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit Taiwan Mazesoba with Karaage due to its saturated fat content from deep-fried karaage and minced pork (approximately 2.1 g saturated fat per 100 g) and its high sodium load, both of which are associated with elevated cardiovascular risk when consumed regularly.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Taiwan Mazesoba with Karaage is a Japanese brothless noodle dish inspired by Taiwanese flavours, served at specialty ramen restaurants. Unlike traditional ramen, mazesoba (literally 'mixed noodles') is served without broth; instead, thick wheat noodles are tossed tableside in a rich, umami-forward sauce made from soy-based tare, chili oil, garlic, and rendered pork fat. The standard bowl is topped with seasoned minced pork, a raw egg yolk, nori (dried seaweed), green onions, bamboo shoots (menma), and sesame seeds. In this variant, karaage — Japanese-style deep-fried chicken marinated in soy sauce, mirin, and ginger, then coated in potato starch and fried to a crispy golden exterior — is served alongside or atop the noodles, adding a substantial protein component and contrasting texture. The dish is rich, savoury, and filling, with a complex flavour profile combining umami, mild heat, and the nuttiness of sesame. Nutritionally, it is a calorie-dense meal estimated at approximately 198 kcal per 100 g, with moderate protein (~11.5 g/100 g) from the chicken and pork, moderate fat (~8.2 g/100 g) from frying and chili oil, and a significant carbohydrate contribution (~20.5 g/100 g) from the wheat noodles. Sodium content is high (~520 mg/100 g) due to the soy-based sauce, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at 55 (medium), with a high glycemic load per standard serving, relevant for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. Taiwan Mazesoba originated in Nagoya, Japan, and has spread to ramen restaurants across Asia, including Singapore, where it is served at establishments such as Ramen Matsuri.
common_user_queries: Is mazesoba the same as ramen?
How many calories in Taiwan Mazesoba with Karaage?
Is Taiwan Mazesoba high in sodium?
What is the difference between mazesoba and ramen?

confidence_reason: No single approved primary source entry exists for Taiwan Mazesoba with Karaage as a composite restaurant dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for cooked wheat noodles, Japanese-style fried chicken, minced pork, soy sauce, and chili oil. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked wheat noodles as the closest analogous approved source. Confidence is medium due to composite estimation and absence of a direct dish-level measurement in any approved primary source.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reliably reported in the USDA FoodData Central component entries used for this composite estimation. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the component source data used for this estimation. All other -1 values in per_100g have been replaced with estimated numeric values where defensible component data was available. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on cooked wheat noodles from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Taiwan Mazesoba with Karaage exists in any approved source. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (55 x 102.5) / 100 = 56.4, where 102.5 g is the estimated carbohydrate content of a 500 g serving. The input record noted 'Soup comes with all Mazesoba' — this is interpreted as a side soup accompaniment, which is standard for mazesoba restaurants; the dish itself remains brothless. Sodium estimate reflects the soy-heavy sauce profile typical of Taiwan Mazesoba and is a key clinical concern. This record was served by Ramen Matsuri at 7 North Canal Road, Singapore, as sourced from GrabFood Singapore (price SGD 21.04); restaurant-sourced data is not used as a nutrient evidence source per Project Veritas source policy.
